data.title: Mercer signals $120M mass timber revenue for 2026 while advancing cost reduction targets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mercer International (MERC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Juan Bueno stated that operating results "continue to be undermined by significant market headwinds" and noted the recognition of noncash asset impairments, including a major write-down at the Peace River mill. He emphasized that underlying operational performance improved quarter-over-quarter, with cost reductions and efficiency improvements contributing to a $54 million increase in liquidity. Bueno highlighted the "One Goal One Hundred" program, which delivered about $30 million in cost savings for 2025, and reaffirmed the target of $100 million in improvements by the end of 2026.
  • Bueno reported progress on the carbon capture demonstration unit at the Peace River mill with Svante Technologies: "the pilot plant is operating and the results so far are very encouraging, both in terms of the efficiency as well as purity of the CO2 being captured."
  • Bueno described a "healthy order book" for the mass timber business, with the order backlog at $163 million compared to $80 million at the end of Q3, and projected 2026 mass timber revenue of about $120 million.
  • CFO Richard Short stated, "our operating EBITDA for the fourth quarter was negative $20 million, up $8 million when compared to the third quarter's results." He reported a $216 million noncash impairment charge, primarily linked to Peace River, and a $12 million impairment in the solid wood segment. Short also noted, "in the fourth quarter, our aggregate liquidity improved by over $54 million to $430 million, comprised of about $187 million of cash and $243 million of undrawn revolvers."

Outlook

  • Management expects "modest NBSK and NBHK price improvements in Q1 in both Europe and China, while North America is expected to be stable."
  • Bueno projected 2026 mass timber revenue "to be about $120 million," with expectations that "revenues to be more than double what we had in 2025 or above $120 million."
  • Planned capital expenditures for 2026 are forecasted at $60 million to $80 million, focused on maintenance, environmental, and safety projects.
  • The company targets "almost 50 days less planned maintenance downtime compared to 2025."

Financial Results

  • Mercer reported a consolidated net loss of $309 million for Q4 or $4.61 per share, including $239 million in noncash impairments, compared to a net loss of $81 million or $1.21 per share in Q3.
  • Operating EBITDA for Q4 was negative $20 million, representing an $8 million improvement from Q3.
  • Pulp sales volumes increased by 20,000 tons to 472,000 tons, while pulp production was stable at 460,000 tons.
  • Mass timber revenues grew roughly 6% compared to Q3, and the order book reached $163 million at quarter end.
  • Aggregate liquidity rose by $54 million to $430 million.

Q&A

  • Roger Spitz, BofA Securities, asked about maintenance covenant headroom and working capital outflow; Short responded, "we're comfortable that we're well under the covenants at the end of the quarter, but we will expect them to get tighter as the year progresses... It will be a net outflow probably around $100 million to $150 million at this point."
  • Sean Steuart, TD Cowen, inquired about asset recycling and potential closures; Bueno replied, "the possibility of claiming reasonable value for any particular asset in this cycle is not the right time. But we are obviously looking into that as we focus on our core assets and see our way through this trough."
  • Edward Brucker, Barclays, questioned the future of Peace River; Bueno stated, "what we're working on is our transition from less hardwood to more softwood. That is center and foremost... by the end of this year, we expect it to be 50-50."
  • Cole Hathorn, Jefferies, explored fiber cost inflation in Germany; Bueno detailed how German energy policy and reduced harvesting have increased wood chip costs, adding, "that's why we're expecting fiber prices to increase until we see proper harvest levels come back and after we get through the seasonal aspect of bio pellets and biofuels."
  • Dominic Gerster, Aperture Investors, asked about RCF extensions; Short said, "the conversations are going well... before the end of Q2 for sure."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised persistent concerns about liquidity, asset sales, and cost inflation, maintaining a neutral-to-cautious tone, with repeated questions on working capital outflow and asset strategies.
  • Management maintained a measured, slightly defensive tone when discussing impairments and cost pressures but was more confident regarding growth in mass timber and cost savings, as Bueno stated, "we're confident in this business being a growth engine for Mercer in the short term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited slightly more caution due to large impairment charges and ongoing market headwinds, but optimism was higher around mass timber and cost control.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net loss increased sharply due to noncash impairments in Q4, while EBITDA loss improved by $8 million from Q3.
  • Liquidity position improved by $54 million in Q4 versus Q3, attributed to working capital management and cost reductions.
  • Mass timber backlog doubled quarter-over-quarter, with management projecting significant revenue growth in 2026.
  • Management’s tone in Q4 was more focused on liquidity and asset rebalancing, while Q3 emphasized initial progress on cost structure improvements and the early stages of the One Goal One Hundred program.
  • Analysts’ questions remained focused on liquidity and asset strategy, but showed heightened concern about working capital outflow and large impairment charges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing market headwinds, pricing weakness, and elevated fiber costs in Germany and Canada as significant challenges.
  • Trade uncertainty and tariffs remain major overhangs, especially with upcoming CUSMA negotiations.
  • Management expects fiber costs to "increase meaningfully for both our pulp and sawmill businesses" in Q1 2026.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the potential for further asset impairments, working capital outflows, and the timing of asset sales or closures.

Final Takeaway

Management is navigating a challenging market marked by continued pricing weakness, rising fiber costs, and significant noncash asset impairments, most notably at the Peace River mill. Despite these headwinds, the company improved its liquidity through active cost and working capital management, advanced its One Goal One Hundred cost reduction program, and reported robust growth in its mass timber business, projecting revenues above $120 million for 2026. The company remains focused on liquidity, prudent capital allocation, and strategic transformation toward diversified, sustainable revenue streams amid persistent industry uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amwell outlines path to cash flow breakeven in Q4 2026 with high-quality revenue focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Well Corporation (AMWL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Ido Schoenberg, Chairman & CEO, described 2025 as a “pivotal year” marked by a major transformation and a sharpened focus on offering what he called “the best tech platform in the market.” He stated, “We divested non-core activities. The sale of APC is one example. We restructured our company and dramatically reduced our cost base. We realigned our road map and go-to-market investments.” Schoenberg emphasized that 2026 begins with “clear visibility towards the goal of cash flow breakeven from operations in Q4.”
  • Schoenberg highlighted the company’s strategic shift, saying, “Our 2026 top line is smaller, but now it's primarily high-quality, high upside, sticky revenue. This gives us clear visibility to reach our cash flow breakeven goal in Q4 of this year.” He noted that Amwell is now “building deeper, long-term relationships with the payers, government and health systems.”
  • Mark Hirschhorn, Executive VP, COO & CFO, reported, “Total revenue for the year was $249.3 million. Importantly, subscription revenue continued to become a larger and more durable component of our business, representing 53% of total revenue, up from 45% in 2024.”
  • Hirschhorn also emphasized cost control and profitability progress: “We reduced both net loss and adjusted EBITDA losses by approximately $100 million each, driven by disciplined cost actions and a more focused operating model.”

Outlook

  • Hirschhorn provided guidance for 2026: “For the full year 2026, we expect revenue in the range of $195 million to $205 million. We expect AMG visits between 1.32 million and 1.37 million visits. Adjusted EBITDA loss in the range of $24 million to $18 million.” For Q1 2026, “we expect revenue in the range of $48 million to $53 million, and an adjusted EBITDA loss in the range of $7 million to $5 million.”
  • Hirschhorn stated, “Based on our current outlook and continued execution, we expect the company to achieve positive cash flow from operations in the fourth quarter of this year.”

Financial Results

  • Hirschhorn reported, “Total revenue in the quarter was $55.3 million, representing a 22.1% year-over-year decline. Subscription revenue was $28.8 million, down 22% year-over-year.” He attributed the decline to “the step down in our DHA contract this past summer, churn that occurred earlier in 2024 and to a lesser extent, our reprioritization of certain parts of the business to focus on our core payer and government markets.”
  • Amwell Medical Group visit revenue was $23.7 million. Paid AMG visits were flat at approximately 340,000 visits in the quarter. Total platform visits were 1 million, down 28.4% year-over-year.
  • Cost of goods sold was $27 million, resulting in gross profit of $28.3 million. Gross margin was 51.2%, down 280 basis points year-over-year. Operating expenses, including depreciation and amortization, were $55.3 million, a 30.7% reduction year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was a loss of $10.3 million, improving from a loss of $12.7 million in the previous quarter. Net loss was $25.2 million compared to $30.7 million in the third quarter.
  • Cash burn for the quarter was approximately $19 million, with $182 million in cash and marketable securities and no debt at year-end.

Q&A

  • Corey DeVito, Wells Fargo Securities: Asked about contract renewal timelines and government opportunities. Schoenberg responded, “In 2025, we signed 50 contracts, most of which are renewals… The amount of what we renewed in '26 are significantly lower with one important exception, which is the DHA renewal that we expect to have this summer.”
  • Jailendra Singh, Truist Securities: Inquired about the competitive landscape with new AI entrants. Schoenberg answered, “The integration of those AI programs and the ability to integrate, switch and maintain multiple AI programs turned out to be a very big challenge for our customers… our platform would be an important utility as our clients do that.”
  • David Larsen, BTIG: Asked about headwinds, tailwinds, and specifics on the DHA renewal. Hirschhorn said, “The most likely tailwind would be an earlier adoption of our technology-enabled platform… We do, of course, have a renewal with the DHA this summer. We're extremely confident that, that will be renewed again and hopefully for a longer term.”
  • Craig Hettenbach, Morgan Stanley: Queried about long-term growth prospects with a smaller, higher quality revenue base. Schoenberg highlighted, “We believe that the same-store growth presents a very meaningful revenue opportunity for us… the government sector represents an incredible growth opportunity as well.”
  • Eric Percher, Nephron Research: Asked about revenue quality and divestitures. Schoenberg replied, “We divested APC, and we deemphasized other areas that are non-core. Essentially, our one product right now is still a very good match to all the market segments…”
  • Ryan MacDonald, Needham & Company: Sought insight on renewal discussions informing go-to-market strategy. Schoenberg shared, “All of our renewals… were related to the same offering to the new Amwell Platform. And in many ways, while they are technically renewals… you can consider them in many ways, a new sale or almost a net new sale.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on renewal risks, adoption of AI, and long-term growth clarity. The tone was generally neutral, with some probing on competitive threats and contract renewals.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, repeatedly referencing cost controls, subscription durability, and “clear visibility” to cash flow breakeven. Hirschhorn stressed, “We expect the company to achieve positive cash flow from operations in the fourth quarter of this year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted to greater confidence and clarity about the company’s direction, whereas analyst questions remained consistently focused on core business risks and long-term growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw management further streamline operations, complete divestitures, and shift to a single core platform offering. This contrasts with the previous quarter’s emphasis on broad product integration and early-stage divestitures.
  • Guidance for 2026 was more explicit, with management forecasting revenue between $195 million and $205 million and a target for positive cash flow in Q4, compared to previous guidance focused on cost containment and broad operational efficiency.
  • Analyst focus remained on contract renewals, particularly the DHA, and the long-term impacts of AI integration, similar to the prior quarter.
  • Margins and operating expenses continued to improve, with a more significant drop in operating expenses this quarter.
  • Management’s confidence in hitting cash flow breakeven has grown, and there is a clearer articulation of the strategic shift to high-quality, recurring revenue.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified continued churn, the need to renew the DHA contract, and the challenge of integrating rapidly evolving AI programs as ongoing risks.
  • Hirschhorn noted the potential revenue impact if the DHA contract is not renewed or expanded, stating that adding back previously eliminated digital behavioral health and automated care programs to the DHA contract would be “material.”
  • Analysts pressed on potential headwinds to guidance, competitive threats from new AI entrants, and clarity around revenue quality as non-core assets are exited.
  • Management responded by emphasizing the high renewal rate, the shift to a single platform, and ongoing cost discipline as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Amwell’s leadership emphasized that the company enters 2026 with a streamlined business model centered on a single, high-quality technology platform, a strong recurring revenue base, and no debt. Management stated that strategic divestitures and a focus on core payer and government markets position the company to achieve cash flow breakeven from operations in the fourth quarter of 2026, while continuing to invest in AI-driven innovation, deepen client relationships, and pursue meaningful growth opportunities in both same-store and government sectors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bridgewater Associates adds new stakes in CAT, DELL, exits T, NLY, among Q4 moves
data.description:
Bridgewater Associates, the hedge fund manager founded by billionaire investor Ray Dalio, took new positions in Caterpillar (CAT), with about 62.2K shares, Dell Technologies (DELL), with 156.3K shares, and Spotify Technology (SPOT), with 23.5K shares, during Q4 2025, according to a U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filing released on Friday.

Conversely, the firm closed out its holdings in AT&T (T), Annaly Capital Management (NLY), Affirm Holdings (AFRM) and Aehr test Systems (AEHR.

Increased stakes include Nvidia (NVDA), to 3.87M shares in Q4 from 2.51M shares in the prior quarter, Amazon (AMZN), to 1.95M from 1.12M, and Mastercard (MA), to 409.2K from 366.3K, the 13F filing showed.

Trimmed stakes: Salesforce (CRM), to 1.93M shares in Q4 from 2.01M in Q3, Microsoft (MSFT), to 984K from 1.10M, and Wells Fargo (WFC), to 1.14M from 2.54M.

data.title: China's supercharged gold trading looks like 'speculative bubble,' Capital Economics says
data.description:
China's recent gold trading frenzy appears more like a speculative bubble than a typical flight to safe-haven assets, raising the risk of more market turbulence, Capital Economics analysts said Friday.

"The growing use of leverage and futures trading to gain exposure to gold in China suggests that the recent increase in Chinese gold demand is more consistent with a speculative bubble inflating," analyst Hamad Hussain said in a note.

Private Chinese investor demand is a major driver of gold prices, fueling greater use of gold-linked financial products, and the increase in gold warrants on the Shanghai Futures Exchange also indicates that futures trading has become a bigger part of China's gold market, Hussain explained.

The continuing property downturn should support safe-haven demand for gold in China in the medium term, Hussain added, but he also noted that Chinese investors are increasingly using leverage to gain exposure to gold, which could boost market volatility and the likelihood of sharp price swings like those seen earlier this year.

Gold and silver prices jumped on Friday, as weaker-than-expected U.S. inflation results reignited hopes for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year, offsetting concerns from stronger-than-expected jobs data earlier in the week.

"The broader backdrop remains one of elevated volatility following this week's sharp liquidation across precious metals, but today's move suggests the correction may have overshot, with bargain hunting and position adjustments now providing support," ING commodity strategist Ewa Manthey said in a note.

On Friday, front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for February delivery closed +2% to $5,022.00/oz, and front-month Comex February silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended +3% to $77.851/oz.

Gold and silver both rose 1.4% this week; gold has gained in eight out of the past 10 weeks, and silver has finished higher in 11 out of the past 15 weeks.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Bank of America pays CEO Brian Moynihan $41M for 2025
data.description:
Bank of America (BAC) increased the total 2025 compensation for Chair and CEO Brian Moynihan by 17% from $35M in 2024, the company disclosed in a filing on Friday.

The board considered the company's 2025 performance, including 13% net income growth, 7% revenue growth, its balance sheet strength, expense management, low net charge-off ratio, and shareholder returns in determining his compensation.

During 2025, Bank of America (BAC) stock rose 25%, compared with the S&P 500's 16% increase.

His annual base salary is $1.5M, consistent with previous years. He received no cash bonus. The total value of his 2025 equity incentive award is $39.5M, made up of 30% of cash-settled restricted stock units that will vest over the next 12 months, 20% of stock-settled RSUs that will vest annually over the next four years, and 50% of stock-settled performance RSUs.

data.title: X3 Holdings faces Nasdaq delisting over sub-$1 share price
data.description:
• X3 Holdings Co (XTKG) received a Nasdaq determination letter on February 10, citing non-compliance with the $1 minimum bid price requirement.
  • Although Nasdaq typically provides a 180-day compliance period, X3 is not eligible due to its one-for-six reverse stock split completed on December 30, 2025.

  • Nasdaq has determined to delist the company’s securities from The Nasdaq Capital Market.

  • Unless X3 requests an appeal by February 17, 2026, trading will be suspended at market open on February 19, 2026.

  • XTKG shares down 5% post-market.

data.title: Disc Medicine plunges on FDA Complete Response Letter for bitopertin
data.description:
• Disc Medicine (IRON) closed down ~22% Friday after receiving a Complete Response Letter from the US FDA for its application for bitopertin as a treatment for erythropoietic protoporphyria.
  • The agency noted it will need to see the results of the phase 3 APOLLO trial before making a final decision. Topline data is expected in Q4.
  • Disc's NDA included data from the phase 2 AURORA and BEACON trials. The company noted that the FDA found the results from these trials indicated bitopertin led to significantly lowered levels of PPIX, the molecule in the skin that causes the condition.
  • Bitopertin received accelerated approval review and is one of the candidates under the FDA Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher pilot program.
  • Erythropoietic protoporphyria is a rare, inherited condition characterized by severe skin photosensitivity.

data.title: Sabra outlines 4.9% to 5.4% FFO growth for 2026 while accelerating SHOP investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sabra Health Care REIT, Inc. (SBRA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Rick Matros opened by highlighting that "Sabra's NOI growth for the SHOP portfolio, excluding our transition facilities, is expected to be sturdy in 2026 as it has been in 2025, and we expect the transition facilities as they continue to improve to add to the overall growth in our SHOP performance." Matros noted that the company's guidance of 4.9% and 5.4% growth at the midpoint for normalized FFO and normalized AFFO, respectively, reflects ongoing execution of strategy. He reported the investment pipeline remains robust, with $450 million completed in 2025 and $240 million in awarded deals expected to close in early 2026. Matros emphasized, "Our expectation is that we will materially exceed the volume of 2025 investments and are clearly off to a strong start in 2026."
  • Matros emphasized operational performance across the portfolio, including "strong occupancy gains and increased cash NOI margins" in the SHOP segment, margin improvement in same-store Senior Housing, and all-time high rent coverage for skilled nursing. Leverage was reported as steady at the 5x target, and the regulatory environment remains stable.
  • Darrin Smith, Chief Investment Officer, reported sequential revenue growth of 15.8% and cash NOI growth of 18.4% with a margin expansion of 60 basis points in the managed Senior Housing portfolio. Smith stated, "During the quarter, Sabra invested over $150 million, adding 4 properties to Sabra's managed portfolio, bringing total year investments to roughly $450 million with an estimated initial cash yield of 7.5%."
  • CFO Michael Costa reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, we recognized normalized FFO per share of $0.36, and normalized AFFO per share of $0.38. In absolute dollars, normalized FFO and normalized AFFO totaled $91.2 million and $95.2 million this quarter, respectively."

Outlook

  • Costa introduced full-year 2026 guidance: Net income per share of $0.60 to $0.64, FFO and normalized FFO per share of $1.49 to $1.53, and AFFO and normalized AFFO per share of $1.55 to $1.59. He stated, "At the midpoint, we expect both normalized FFO per share and normalized AFFO per share to increase approximately 5% over 2025."
  • Costa explained that guidance assumes cash NOI growth for the triple-net portfolio in the low single digits, with same-store managed Senior Housing portfolio cash NOI growth in the low to mid-teens.
  • Guidance does not assume any 2026 investment, disposition, or capital markets activities that have not yet been completed.

Financial Results

  • Costa reported that cash NOI from the triple-net portfolio decreased $1.3 million from the third quarter, while managed Senior Housing portfolio cash NOI increased $5.5 million, leading to a net sequential increase of $4.2 million. Managed Senior Housing portfolio cash NOI totaled $35.6 million in Q4 compared to $30.1 million in Q3.
  • Interest and other income was $10.6 million for the quarter, down from $12.7 million last quarter. Cash interest expense was $26.6 million, consistent with the previous quarter. Cash G&A was $12.5 million, up from $9.1 million, with the increase attributed to performance-based compensation.
  • Costa highlighted a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 5.00x as of December 31, 2025, and a permanent debt cost of 3.92% with a weighted average remaining term of 4.2 years. Liquidity stood at approximately $1.2 billion.
  • The Board declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.30 per share, representing a payout of 79% of normalized AFFO per share.

Q&A

  • William John Kilichowski, Wells Fargo: Asked about building blocks for same-store growth and long-term prospects. Darrin Smith responded, "We expect to see continued occupancy growth...we fully expect our portfolio to get into the low 90s."
  • Kilichowski asked about the maturity of the RCA loan and capital redeployment. Matros responded that the loan is being serviced and guidance assumes the lease stays in place.
  • Juan Sanabria, BMO: Asked about CapEx expectations. Smith estimated non-maintenance CapEx in 2026 to be in the $20 million to $30 million range.
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Inquired about occupancy targets. Matros said, "We think we can exceed 90%. Once you get to the mid-90s, you're kind of effectively full."
  • Austin Wurschmidt, KeyBanc: Asked about transition assets' contribution to NOI. Costa explained that transition assets are currently lagging but expected to improve and provide additional NOI uplift.
  • Seth Bergey, Citi: Asked about investment mix and competitive landscape. Costa confirmed $240 million of awarded transactions is significantly weighted towards SHOP, with return expectations unchanged.
  • Michael Stroyeck, Green Street: Asked about non-same-store SHOP asset growth. Smith stated newer assets still have room for NOI growth before inclusion in the same-store pool.
  • Farrell Granath, BofA: Asked about acquisition occupancy levels and NOI margin potential. Matros said assets are typically acquired at 86-87% occupancy, providing room for growth.
  • Alec Feygin, Baird: Asked about deal flow competition and development lending. Costa noted cap rate compression but continued access to quality assets and described a preferred equity program for development.
  • Omotayo Okusanya, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about skilled nursing regulatory outlook. Matros responded that rate increases are tapering but remain robust, with no state-level concerns.
  • Richard Anderson, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about SHOP operational advantages. Matros highlighted Sabra's experienced asset management team and continuous process improvement.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was inquisitive and focused on long-term growth drivers, risk management, and portfolio composition, with a neutral to slightly positive sentiment. Questions sought clarification on occupancy, investment pipeline, and operational execution.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing robust performance and future growth. During Q&A, responses remained consistent, providing detailed explanations and affirming guidance, with Matros and Costa displaying steady assurance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts maintained a similar focus but with increased attention to execution and future growth runway. Management's confidence remained steady, reinforced by strong operational and financial trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 introduces a 4.9% to 5.4% growth target for normalized FFO and AFFO, compared to reaffirmed 2025 guidance in the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus remains on expanding the SHOP portfolio, with investment activity accelerating from $450 million in 2025 to expectations of surpassing that in 2026.
  • Analysts' questions continue to focus on occupancy, investment mix, and operational execution, while management maintains confidence and provides more granularity on strategic initiatives and guidance assumptions.
  • Key metrics such as managed Senior Housing occupancy, margin improvement, and pipeline growth continue to be highlighted, with operational performance sustaining momentum.

Risks and Concerns

  • Matros noted that guidance does not assume new investments beyond those completed or awarded, potentially limiting upside if pipeline deals are delayed.
  • Smith acknowledged that non-maintenance CapEx could range from $20 million to $30 million, indicating continued reinvestment needs.
  • Analyst concerns centered on transition assets' lag in performance, regulatory risks in skilled nursing, and competitive pressures in asset acquisitions. Management emphasized stable regulatory environment and disciplined investment approach.

Final Takeaway

Sabra Health Care REIT enters 2026 with robust operational momentum, guided by a strong investment pipeline and a focus on expanding its SHOP portfolio. Management projects mid-single-digit growth in normalized FFO and AFFO, supported by occupancy gains, margin expansion, and disciplined capital deployment, while maintaining a stable regulatory outlook and substantial liquidity. The company expects continued growth in both organic and external avenues, with a clear path to exceeding prior investment volumes and reinforcing its strategic positioning in senior housing and healthcare real estate.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Baupost adds Amazon, exits Restaurant Brands, boosts UNP stake, among Q4 trades
data.description:
Seth Klarman's Baupost hedge fund added Amazon (AMZN), exited its stake in Restaurant Brands International, and increased its holdings in Union Pacific (UNP), among other Q4 tradings disclosed in its 13 filing.

The fund added 2.12M shares of Amazon (AMNZ) valued at $489.7M, 625K shares of  Molina Healthcare (MOH) ($108.5M), and 4.86M shares of Grupo Aeromexico (AERO) ($106.6M).

It closed its holdings in Restaurant Brands International (QSR).

The firm increased its holdings in Union Pacific (UNP) to 1.63M shares ($376.4M) from 1.50M shares in the prior quarter; Fidelity National Information (FIS) to 4.50M ($299.3M) shares from 3.79M; and Fiserv (FI) to 2.20M shares ($147.8M) from 895K shares.

During the quarter, Baupost reduced its stakes in Alphabet (GOOG) class C shares to 1.09M shares ($341.2M) from 1.86M shares; CRH (CRH) to 1.08M shares ($113.9M) from 3.83M shares; and Liberty Global (LBTYA) class A shares to 2.08M ($23.2M) from 5.08M shares, among others, according to the filing.

data.title: Visionary Holdings secures $450,000 in debt financing
data.description:
• Visionary Holdings (GV) has entered into a securities purchase agreement with an existing institutional investor, under which it has issued to the Investor a senior secured convertible promissory note in the principal amount of $500,000 in exchange for gross proceeds of $450,000.
  • Previously the company had issued to the investor a senior secured convertible promissory note in the principal amount of $1.50M pursuant to a securities purchase agreement dated October 2, 2024.
  • GV +6.09% after hours to $1.22.
  • Source: Press Release
